## Join us on the

# Fighting Oligarchy  Tour

Over the course of the next few weeks, I'll be hitting the road to have real discussions across America on how we move forward to take on the Oligarchs and corporate interests who have so much power and influence in this country.

RSVP to an event near you below, or tune in live to watch from home.

Next Stop

## Help fund the Fighting Oligarchy Tour.

Over the coming weeks, Bernie is going to be engaging in the struggle to determine where we go from here. That will take a modest amount of resources to travel, organize, hold events, and create content that reaches people where they are.

Make a donation today to help fund the Fighting Oligarchy Tour.

# Fighting Oligarchy: Where We Go From Here with Bernie Sanders in Detroit With Special Guest Dr. Abdul El-Sayed

**When:**
Sun., May 3, 4 p.m.



No cover

This Sunday, May 3rd, U.S. Senate candidate Dr. Abdul El-Sayed will join Senator Bernie Sanders (I-Vt.) in Detroit as part of the Fighting Oligarchy tour. Abdul was the first candidate endorsed by Sen. Sanders in the 2026 midterm cycle, with the Senator backing him on the first day of his campaign. This is the Senator’s second trip to Michigan in support of Abdul after drawing a large crowd at a rally in Kalamazoo last August.

Bernie Sanders arrives in Detroit on May 3 with a sharp political agenda and a word the market is betting heavily on: AI. The senator’s ‘Fighting Oligarchy: Where We Go From Here’ tour has made artificial intelligence a recurring theme alongside corporate power, and traders have priced that pattern at 89.5%. That is not cautious speculation. That is near-consensus.

The Detroit rally pairs Sanders with Michigan U.S. Senate candidate Dr. Abdul El-Sayed, the first candidate Sanders endorsed in the 2026 midterm cycle. Sanders drew over 10,000 people at a prior Michigan stop in Warren in March 2025. The Detroit event builds directly on that momentum, with oligarchic control over the economy as the stated frame. AI fits that frame tightly. The math doesn’t lie: 89.5% is a market that has made up its mind.

## How the Sanders Detroit Contract Works

This contract resolves YES if Bernie Sanders says ‘AI’ or ‘Artificial Intelligence’ during his live-streamed or broadcast remarks in Detroit on May 3, 2026. Only remarks broadcast or streamed live count toward resolution. The resolution source is market judgment based on the broadcast record.

- YES (AI / Artificial Intelligence mentioned): $0.90, implied probability 89.5%
- NO (AI / Artificial Intelligence not mentioned): $0.11, implied probability 10.5%

The contract falls to NO if Sanders delivers his entire Detroit speech without once saying ‘AI’ or ‘Artificial Intelligence’ on a live broadcast. Sanders keeps a tight rhetorical focus on oligarchy, billionaires, and working families. A Detroit speech that omits AI entirely and leans exclusively on healthcare, corruption, or billionaire framing is the only path to a NO payout. Given his recent Senate op-ed framing AI as an existential threat to the working class, that omission would be a significant departure from his current message.

## Market Signals: Stable Conviction, No Pressure

Momentum on the Sanders Detroit AI contract is flat at +0.0% over one hour, with no 24-hour change recorded and a trend score of 42.67. That composite reads as locked-in conviction, not drift. Sanders has made AI a centerpiece of his ‘Fighting Oligarchy’ messaging, and traders see no catalyst that would shift that pattern.

Total volume sits at $1,316, with $5,033 in available liquidity. For a single-event binary contract resolving in under 24 hours, that liquidity-to-volume ratio signals a market that is confident but lightly traded. Thin volume can mean price moves fast on new information, but no new information has arrived.

- Bernie Sanders has published a Senate op-ed arguing AI will take working-class jobs, making the word nearly inescapable in his current stump circuit.
- The Fighting Oligarchy tour explicitly ties corporate technology to economic inequality, giving Sanders a natural on-ramp to AI at every stop.
- The 1h change of +0.0% and absent 24h change together confirm no late money is repositioning ahead of the May 3 resolution.
- At 89.5%, the YES price has absorbed the full range of uncertainty this contract allows.
- Liquidity of $5,033 is sufficient to move price if a major last-minute development emerged, but none has.

## Lines Analysis: Sanders and the AI Frame

Sanders enters Detroit with AI already baked into his rhetorical DNA. His Senate op-ed called artificial intelligence the most transformational technology in the history of the world and framed it as a direct threat to working-class jobs. The Fighting Oligarchy tour uses that framing to connect tech billionaires, corporate power, and economic displacement into one coherent argument. Detroit is an auto manufacturing city, and the threat of automation to blue-collar labor is Sanders’s exact target audience. Here’s what the market is missing: the YES case is almost impossible to undermine.

The NO path is narrow but real. Sanders could keep Detroit focused on healthcare costs, the billionaire class without naming AI, or the El-Sayed Senate race. If the event runs short, if the broadcast cuts out early, or if Sanders pivots entirely to electoral politics, the AI mention could fall away. That scenario gets 10.5%, and the market has generously priced in that edge-case risk.

- Any clip of Sanders mentioning AI or automation in Detroit moves YES toward 95%+ instantly.
- A broadcast disruption or truncated stream keeps NO alive but does not make it likely.
- El-Sayed’s Senate campaign focus could pull Sanders toward Michigan-specific healthcare and housing framing.
- A viral pre-rally statement from Sanders on AI would lock YES before the first speech word is spoken.
- Late movement in the NO price above $0.15 would signal traders have information about the rally’s expected agenda.

The $1,316 in volume reflects a low-dollar, high-conviction contract. The data favors YES. Sanders’s own published record makes AI omission the outlier scenario, not the base case.

**LINES VERDICT**

**AI Mention Confirmed by Pattern**

Sanders has built his entire 2026 tour around AI as an oligarchic threat to working families. A Detroit speech without that word would contradict his own Senate record and his Fighting Oligarchy brand.

**What the market says:** 89.5% confidence that Sanders says AI or Artificial Intelligence in Detroit on May 3. The contract resolves in hours. With flat momentum and no repositioning signals, the market has settled. Any live-stream disruption before resolution is the only real source of last-minute volatility before the May 3, 2026 deadline.

## Political Context: Sanders, AI, and the 2026 Midterm Frame

Sanders endorsed El-Sayed on day one of his Senate campaign, making Detroit a dual-purpose stop: tour messaging and electoral investment. El-Sayed is running in a three-way primary against Chuck Schumer-backed Haley Stevens and Mallory McMorrow. Sanders’s presence signals a proxy war between the progressive and establishment wings of the Democratic Party.

Sanders’s op-ed described AI as controlled by oligarchs who want to replace workers entirely, not just automate specific tasks. That framing slots directly into the Fighting Oligarchy tour’s Detroit stop. Detroit’s industrial identity makes AI-driven job displacement an emotionally resonant message, not just a policy talking point.

Before May 3, 2026, watch for any pre-rally statements from Sanders, any El-Sayed campaign press releases naming rally themes, or any broadcast confirmation of a livestream host. Those signals would move this market faster than anything else.

## FAQ

- What does 89.5% mean here? Traders collectively assign a roughly nine-in-ten chance that Sanders says AI or Artificial Intelligence during his live Detroit remarks on May 3.
- What happens if Sanders says neither ‘AI’ nor ‘Artificial Intelligence’? The NO contract pays out at $1.00, and YES holders lose their stake. That outcome carries roughly a one-in-ten implied probability.
- What moves the price between now and resolution? Any credible signal about the rally’s agenda, a broadcast cancellation, or a last-minute shift in Sanders’s stump focus could push price in either direction quickly given thin volume.
- When does this market resolve? The contract resolves on May 3, 2026, after Sanders’s Detroit remarks have been broadcast or streamed live and the record can be reviewed.
- Is $1,316 in volume enough to trust the price? For a short-duration binary market, low volume with stable price movement reflects consensus, not apathy. The $5,033 in liquidity means a large trade could still shift the price before close.

### What Could Shift These Probabilities?

#### AI Mention Supporting Factors

Sanders's own Senate op-ed called AI the most transformational technology in history and framed it as a working-class threat. The Fighting Oligarchy tour ties that message to corporate power at every stop. Detroit's manufacturing identity makes automation displacement the perfect local hook. YES touches 95%+ the moment Sanders says the word on a live stream.

#### AI Mention Risk Factors

A rally focused tightly on El-Sayed's Senate race could crowd out national policy messaging. Sanders might lean on healthcare costs, billionaire taxation, or Michigan-specific housing affordability without naming AI directly. If the broadcast runs short or coverage is interrupted before Sanders reaches his technology remarks, YES takes an unexpected hit.

#### NO Contract Comeback Scenario

NO pays out only if Sanders delivers his entire broadcast remarks without once saying AI or Artificial Intelligence. That requires Sanders to stay entirely in healthcare, electoral, or billionaire framing for the full event. Given his current op-ed record and tour themes, that would be a deliberate rhetorical choice, not an accident. The NO contract's 10.5% price reflects exactly that: low probability, not zero.

#### Wildcard Factor

A last-minute change in the event format, a broadcast cancellation, or a technical streaming failure could leave the contract unresolvable under its broadcast-only rule. Sanders could also surprise with a speech entirely focused on the Michigan Senate primary dynamics, sidelining his national tour themes. Either scenario scrambles both YES and NO pricing in the final hours before May 3.

**Key macro factor:** Sanders's Fighting Oligarchy tour is running parallel to a Democratic Party civil war between progressive and establishment factions, with the 2026 midterms and the 2028 presidential field both in view.

[Jump to content](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fighting_Oligarchy_tour#bodyContent) From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia 2025–26 series of American political rallies | [![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1f/Alexandria_Ocasio-Cortez_%26_Bernie_Sanders_%2854401274152%29.jpg/330px-Alexandria_Ocasio-Cortez_%26_Bernie_Sanders_%2854401274152%29.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Alexandria_Ocasio-Cortez_%26_Bernie_Sanders_(54401274152).jpg)
[Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Alexandria_Ocasio-Cortez "Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez") (left) and [Bernie Sanders](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bernie_Sanders "Bernie Sanders") (right) on the tour at [Mullett Arena](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mullett_Arena "Mullett Arena") in Tempe, Arizona | | Date | February 22, 2025 – present | | Location | United States | | Type | Political rally tour | | Cause | Opposition to [oligarchy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oligarchy "Oligarchy") and [corporate influence](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Corporatocracy "Corporatocracy") in politics | | Website | [berniesanders.com/fighting-oligarchy-tour](https://berniesanders.com/fighting-oligarchy-tour) | Fighting Oligarchy tour The **Fighting Oligarchy Tour**, sometimes subtitled **Where We Go from Here**,[\[1\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fighting_Oligarchy_tour#cite_note-1) is a series of American [political rallies](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Political_demonstration "Political demonstration") led by [independent](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Independent_politician "Independent politician")[\[a\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fighting_Oligarchy_tour#cite_note-2) U.S. senator [Bernie Sanders](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bernie_Sanders "Bernie Sanders") and [Democratic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)") U.S. representative [Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Alexandria_Ocasio-Cortez "Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez"). It began on February 21, 2025, in [Omaha, Nebraska](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Omaha,_Nebraska "Omaha, Nebraska"), with the stated goal to oppose [oligarchy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oligarchy "Oligarchy") and the influence of billionaires and big corporations in U.S. politics.[\[2\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fighting_Oligarchy_tour#cite_note-snm1-3) A segment covering the western U.S. began on March 20 in [Las Vegas](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Las_Vegas "Las Vegas"), Nevada.[\[3\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fighting_Oligarchy_tour#cite_note-npr1-4) 34,000 people attended a March 21 rally in [Denver](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Denver "Denver"), Colorado, bigger than any event held by the [Democratic Party](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)") at the time, and with a bigger audience than any during Sanders's two presidential campaigns. This was followed by a crowd of 36,000 in [Los Angeles](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Los_Angeles "Los Angeles"), California, on April 12. The tour has featured speeches, musical performances, and efforts to promote policies such as [universal healthcare](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Universal_health_care "Universal health care") and [wealth taxation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wealth_tax "Wealth tax"). ## Background \[ [edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Fighting_Oligarchy_tour&action=edit§ion=1 "Edit section: Background")\] Sanders launched the tour after [Donald Trump](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Donald_Trump "Donald Trump")'s reelection in November 2024, which Sanders cited as evidence of growing corporate influence in governance.[\[4\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fighting_Oligarchy_tour#cite_note-5) Joined by Ocasio-Cortez and other progressive figures, Sanders aimed to address [economic inequality](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Economic_inequality "Economic inequality") and political power dynamics.[\[5\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fighting_Oligarchy_tour#cite_note-pol1-6) ## Themes and messaging \[ [edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Fighting_Oligarchy_tour&action=edit§ion=2 "Edit section: Themes and messaging")\] [![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d7/Senator_Bernie_Sanders_speaking_at_LA_Fighting_Oligarchy_Tour_rally.jpg/250px-Senator_Bernie_Sanders_speaking_at_LA_Fighting_Oligarchy_Tour_rally.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Senator_Bernie_Sanders_speaking_at_LA_Fighting_Oligarchy_Tour_rally.jpg) Senator Bernie Sanders speaking at LA Fighting Oligarchy Tour rally The tour focuses on opposition to [oligarchy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oligarchy "Oligarchy"), characterized as a system where a small, wealthy [elite](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Elite "Elite") holds significant sway over political and economic matters. Speakers address topics such as [income disparity](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Income_distribution "Income distribution"), [corporate lobbying](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lobbying_in_the_United_States "Lobbying in the United States"), and [democratic integrity](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Democracy "Democracy"), advocating reforms including [Medicare for All](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Medicare_for_All "Medicare for All"), a [wealth tax](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wealth_tax "Wealth tax"), and changes to [campaign finance](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Campaign_finance "Campaign finance") laws.[\[6\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fighting_Oligarchy_tour#cite_note-ny1-7) The events also encourage [grassroots](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Grassroots "Grassroots") participation in local and electoral efforts.[\[7\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fighting_Oligarchy_tour#cite_note-8) Other goals include transforming the [Democratic Party](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)") to bring it closer to its " [FDR-era](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Franklin_D._Roosevelt#Presidency_(1933%E2%80%931945) "Franklin D. Roosevelt") roots" in an effort to make it more popular, and pressuring [Republican](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Republican_Party_(United_States) "Republican Party (United States)") lawmakers in battleground districts to reject Republican fiscal plans such as cuts to [Medicaid](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Medicaid "Medicaid") and [tax cuts](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tax_cut "Tax cut") for the wealthy.[\[5\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fighting_Oligarchy_tour#cite_note-pol1-6) ## Tour stops \[ Musical performances have accompanied many of the tour's rallies. [Laura Jane Grace](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Laura_Jane_Grace "Laura Jane Grace") and duo Kinsella & Pulse LLC performed at the March 7 event in Kenosha, Wisconsin, and [Poliça](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Poli%C3%A7a "Poliça") opened for the March 8 event in Altoona. During the March 8 rally in Warren, Michigan, hardcore punk band [the Armed](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Armed "The Armed") performed, beginning their set with a cover of [the Stooges](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Stooges "The Stooges")' " [Search And Destroy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Search_and_Destroy_(The_Stooges_song) "Search and Destroy (The Stooges song)")".[\[76\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fighting_Oligarchy_tour#cite_note-77) Indie rock band [Calexico](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Calexico_(band) "Calexico (band)") introduced the March 22 event in Tucson.[\[6\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fighting_Oligarchy_tour#cite_note-ny1-7) [Neil Young](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Neil_Young "Neil Young") took the stage during the April 12 rally in Los Angeles, which also featured performances by [Jeff Rosenstock](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jeff_Rosenstock "Jeff Rosenstock"), [Dirty Projectors](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dirty_Projectors "Dirty Projectors"), [Indigo De Souza](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indigo_De_Souza "Indigo De Souza"), the Red Pears, and Raise Gospel Choir. Young led a "Take America Back" chant, then played his song " [Rainbow of Colors](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rainbow_of_Colors "Rainbow of Colors")" and a solo rendition of " [Rockin' in the Free World](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rockin%27_in_the_Free_World "Rockin' in the Free World")" on electric guitar, accompanied by [Joan Baez](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Joan_Baez "Joan Baez") and [Maggie Rogers](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Maggie_Rogers "Maggie Rogers").[\[77\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fighting_Oligarchy_tour#cite_note-78) Talia Keys and [I Dont Know How but They Found Me](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/I_Dont_Know_How_but_They_Found_Me "I Dont Know How but They Found Me") played at the April 13 Salt Lake City rally.[\[78\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fighting_Oligarchy_tour#cite_note-79) For his walk-on song, Sanders uses [John Lennon](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/John_Lennon "John Lennon")'s " [Power to the People](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Power_to_the_People_(song) "Power to the People (song)")",[\[79\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fighting_Oligarchy_tour#cite_note-80) as he did in his presidential campaigns of [2016](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bernie_Sanders_2016_presidential_campaign "Bernie Sanders 2016 presidential campaign") and [2020](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bernie_Sanders_2020_presidential_campaign "Bernie Sanders 2020 presidential campaign").[\[80\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fighting_Oligarchy_tour#cite_note-81) ## Reception \[ [edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Fighting_Oligarchy_tour&action=edit§ion=6 "Edit section: Reception")\] [![A large crowd in front of Los Angeles City Hall during Bernie Sanders' Fighting Oligarchy Tour rally on April 12, 2025.](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8c/Bernie_Sanders_Fighting_Oligarchy_Tour_Los_Angeles_Rally.jpg/250px-Bernie_Sanders_Fighting_Oligarchy_Tour_Los_Angeles_Rally.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Bernie_Sanders_Fighting_Oligarchy_Tour_Los_Angeles_Rally.jpg) Crowd gathered outside Los Angeles City Hall for the April 12, 2025, Fighting Oligarchy Tour rally, the largest event of the tour. As of April 12, 2025[\[update\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Fighting_Oligarchy_tour&action=edit), the tour had drawn over 107,000 attendees across various states.[\[81\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fighting_Oligarchy_tour#cite_note-82) According to the ongoing chart of rallies in this article, as of November 17, 2025 the tour has drawn 261,100 attendees. On March 26, [Politico](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Politico "Politico") reported that the Fighting Oligarchy rallies were "bigger than any other events currently being held by Democrats" and that they were acting as a "revival" for [progressives](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Progressivism_in_the_United_States "Progressivism in the United States"). Sanders said that two-thirds of those who registered to attend the events had never gone to see him or donated to him before.[\[5\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fighting_Oligarchy_tour#cite_note-pol1-6) The crowd at the Denver rally was larger than at any event during his [2016](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bernie_Sanders_2016_presidential_campaign "Bernie Sanders 2016 presidential campaign") and [2020](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bernie_Sanders_2020_presidential_campaign "Bernie Sanders 2020 presidential campaign") presidential runs.[\[3\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fighting_Oligarchy_tour#cite_note-npr1-4) These numbers contrasted with low [approval ratings](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Opinion_poll "Opinion poll") for the [Democratic Party](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)").[\[5\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fighting_Oligarchy_tour#cite_note-pol1-6) The tour facilitated grassroots organizing, including volunteer recruitment and support for progressive candidates.[\[82\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fighting_Oligarchy_tour#cite_note-83) Progressives supported the tour for engaging supporters and focusing on corporate influence, while media outlets, including _[The New York Times](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")_, observed similarities to historical protest movements such as [Occupy Wall Street](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Occupy_Wall_Street "Occupy Wall Street").[\[83\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fighting_Oligarchy_tour#cite_note-84) Some members of the Democratic establishment supported the rallies, including some who had previously criticized Sanders; a vice chair of the [Democratic National Committee](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Democratic_National_Committee "Democratic National Committee"), [Malcolm Kenyatta](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Malcolm_Kenyatta "Malcolm Kenyatta"), said: "This is not left versus right. This is about [flight versus fight](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fight-or-flight_response "Fight-or-flight response")." Kenyatta had criticized Sanders in the past.[\[5\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fighting_Oligarchy_tour#cite_note-pol1-6) On May 7, 2025, [Fox News](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fox_News "Fox News") host [Bret Baier](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bret_Baier "Bret Baier") asked Sanders about an April 17, 2025, _[Washington Free Beacon](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Washington_Free_Beacon "The Washington Free Beacon")_ article that said that Sanders's campaign committee had used $221,723 in expenditures in the first quarter of 2025 to charter private jets. Sanders replied that when doing multiple rallies per week, "the only way you can get around" in a timely enough manner is by chartering private jets.[\[84\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fighting_Oligarchy_tour#cite_note-85) ## References \[ [edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Fighting_Oligarchy_tour&action=edit§ion=7 "Edit section: References")\] 01. **[^](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fighting_Oligarchy_tour#cite_ref-1 "Jump up")**Clayton, Victoria (April 13, 2025).
